nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes with sand or fine rubble substrate adjacent to coral structure; typically observed hovering near the entrance of a shared pistol-shrimp burrow at depths of around 15-40 m.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5-8 cm deep
loose coral rubble for burrow construction
open sandy footprint near the front glass for visibility

diet.notes: The Yasha White Ray Shrimp Goby is a carnivore that generally accepts a variety of meaty foods, including frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped table shrimp, and small carnivore pellets or flakes. Feeding at least twice daily in small portions is generally recommended. Newly imported specimens have been reported to take time to accept prepared foods; live or frozen options typically trigger a feeding response first.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, royal grammas)
avoidWith: large aggressive predators (groupers, lionfish, large angelfish)
boisterous bottom-dwellers that disturb the burrow
other shrimp gobies competing for the same substrate territory
triggerfish and other crustacean-hunting species (will eat the partner shrimp)

summary: The Yasha White Ray Shrimp Goby is a small reef fish with bold red bars on a white body and a tall, sail-like dorsal fin. It is best known for its symbiotic partnership with pistol shrimp, sharing a burrow on sandy reef slopes.
careNotes:
The Yasha White Ray Shrimp Goby is a well-regarded symbiotic goby prized for its modest size and vivid red-on-white coloration. In the wild — and readily in captivity — it pairs with a pistol shrimp, most often Randall's Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus randalli), which excavates and maintains a shared burrow while the goby hovers at the entrance keeping watch. Alpheus bellulus, the Tiger Pistol Shrimp, has also been observed pairing with this species in captivity.

Providing a fine sand bed at least 5-8 cm deep, along with a few flat pieces of coral rubble for the pistol shrimp to build under, is generally advised to support this behaviour. Leaving an open sandy area near the front of the aquarium makes the pair easier to observe.

A tightly-fitting lid is important — like most gobies, S. yasha has been reported to jump when startled. The species is generally considered reef-safe, ignoring corals and ornamental invertebrates, though its sand-sifting activity can occasionally bury low-placed coral fragments.

Newly imported specimens have been reported to be slow to accept prepared foods; live or frozen meaty offerings such as mysis shrimp typically elicit a feeding response first, with transition to prepared foods following acclimation. The species is generally rated intermediate in care difficulty for this reason.

Mature males reportedly display a black spot on the pelvic fins. The fish is usually kept singly or as a mated pair. Housing with crustacean-eating predators such as triggerfish should be avoided, as they pose a direct threat to the partner pistol shrimp.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity but is generally considered difficult. Pairs deposit eggs inside the shared burrow and the male tends the clutch. Raising the pelagic larvae requires very small live foods (rotifers, then copepod nauplii) and is the main hurdle for hobbyist breeding success.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page navigated from the gobies collection root provided in the manifest. Primary source for hobby water parameters, tank size minimum, temperament, and diet recommendations.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperament, reef safety, and care level. BlueZoo lists a larger minimum tank (24 gal vs LiveAquaria's 10 gal) and rates care as intermediate vs LiveAquaria's easy; both differences resolved by averaging toward the more cautious figure.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes), wild depth/habitat (15-40 m outer reef slope), maximum standard length (4.7 cm SL), and wild temperature range (24.7-28.9°C). Wild temperature ceiling drove the upper bound of 27°C in the averaged hobby range.

additional.name: Biomes In A Box
additional.notes: Third hobby source confirming water parameters, reef safety, peaceful temperament, sand-bed requirement, and lid recommendation. Notes that males display a black spot on the pelvic fin.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity but rearing pelagic larvae is the main challenge; live rotifers and copepod nauplii are generally required.
